name: Pentose phosphate pathway (hexose monophosphate shunt)
summary: Cytosolic glucose-6-phosphate shunt that generates NADPH for reductive biosynthesis and redox defense, and ribose 5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis, with a non-oxidative branch that reversibly interconverts sugar phosphates back into glycolysis.
description: The pentose phosphate pathway (PPP; phosphogluconate pathway / hexose monophosphate shunt) branches from glycolysis at glucose 6-phosphate and has two functionally distinct segments. The **oxidative phase** (steps 1–3) is irreversible and committed: glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD, the rate-limiting step) and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase each reduce one NADP+, and one carbon is lost as CO2, yielding ribulose 5-phosphate plus 2 NADPH. Net oxidative reaction: glucose 6-phosphate + 2 NADP+ + H2O → ribulose 5-phosphate + CO2 + 2 NADPH + 2 H+. The **non-oxidative phase** (steps 4–8) is fully reversible: ribose-5-phosphate isomerase and ribulose-5-phosphate 3-epimerase generate the pentose pool, then transketolase (thiamine-diphosphate–dependent) and transaldolase reshuffle 3-, 4-, 5- and 7-carbon sugar phosphates, ultimately returning carbon to glycolysis as fructose 6-phosphate and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate. Because the non-oxidative reactions are near-equilibrium, cells route flux according to demand: high NADPH need favors oxidative flux; high ribose need (proliferation) can run the non-oxidative branch in reverse to make ribose from glycolytic intermediates. NADPH supports fatty-acid/sterol synthesis and, via glutathione reductase, maintains reduced glutathione — the pathway is especially active in liver, adipose, lactating mammary gland, adrenal cortex, and red blood cells, where G6PD deficiency (the most common human enzymopathy) causes hemolytic anemia. The oxidative PPP is strongly rewired in cancer: p53 directly inhibits G6PD, while NRF2 transcriptionally induces PPP enzymes to feed antioxidant and anabolic demand.

references.title: p53 regulates biosynthesis through direct inactivation of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase
Nrf2 redirects glucose and glutamine into anabolic pathways in metabolic reprogramming
The return of metabolism: biochemistry and physiology of the pentose phosphate pathway (Stincone et al.)

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Site of all pentose phosphate pathway reactions.

enzymes.name: Glucose-6-phosphate 1-dehydrogenase (G6PD)
enzymes.description: First and rate-limiting enzyme of the oxidative PPP; binds a structural NADP+ that stabilizes the active dimer. Human deficiency is the most common enzymopathy and causes drug/infection-triggered hemolytic anemia.

enzymes.name: 6-Phosphogluconolactonase (6PGL)
enzymes.description: Hydrolyzes the labile 6-phosphoglucono-1,5-lactone to 6-phosphogluconate; the hydrolysis also proceeds slowly non-enzymatically.

enzymes.name: 6-Phosphogluconate dehydrogenase, decarboxylating (6PGD)
enzymes.description: Second NADPH-producing oxidative step; oxidative decarboxylation of 6-phosphogluconate releasing CO2 and yielding ribulose 5-phosphate.

enzymes.name: Ribulose-phosphate 3-epimerase (RPE)
enzymes.description: Divalent-metal-dependent epimerase interconverting ribulose 5-phosphate and xylulose 5-phosphate.

enzymes.name: Transketolase (TKT)
enzymes.description: Thiamine-diphosphate–dependent enzyme that transfers a two-carbon glycolaldehyde unit; catalyzes both non-oxidative transketolase steps.

enzymes.name: Transaldolase (TALDO1)
enzymes.description: Transfers a three-carbon dihydroxyacetone unit via a Schiff-base active-site lysine; no cofactor required.

enzymes.name: Nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (NRF2)
enzymes.description: Master antioxidant-response transcription factor (not a metabolic enzyme). Represented here as a protein regulator node; transcriptionally induces oxidative-PPP enzymes.

enzymes.name: Cellular tumor antigen p53 (TP53)
enzymes.description: Tumor suppressor (not a metabolic enzyme). Represented here as a protein regulator node; cytoplasmic p53 binds and inactivates G6PD.

genes.name: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase gene
genes.description: X-linked gene encoding G6PD; an NRF2 target induced during oxidative stress.

provenance.notes: Written in the physiological non-oxidative direction; KEGG R01641 lists the equation reversed. Reaction is near-equilibrium.
KEGG R01827 specifies beta-D-fructose 6-phosphate (C05345); modeled here as generic D-fructose 6-phosphate (C00085) for cross-pathway consistency with glycolysis.
Written in the physiological non-oxidative direction; KEGG R01067 lists the equation reversed. Reaction is near-equilibrium.

regulations.mechanism: Flux through the oxidative branch is set by the cytosolic NADP+/NADPH ratio. NADPH is a potent inhibitor of G6PD, competitive with the NADP+ substrate, so accumulating NADPH throttles the committed step; consumption of NADPH by biosynthesis and glutathione regeneration lowers the ratio and relieves the inhibition.

NRF2 (NFE2L2), the master antioxidant-response transcription factor, binds antioxidant/electrophile response elements and induces oxidative-PPP genes — G6PD together with PGD, TKT and TALDO1 — redirecting glucose into the pathway to supply NADPH and ribose during oxidative stress and PI3K–Akt-driven proliferation in cancer.

Cytoplasmic wild-type p53 binds G6PD and prevents formation of the catalytically active dimer, suppressing oxidative-PPP flux, NADPH production and biosynthesis. Tumour-associated p53 mutants lose this activity, de-repressing the PPP and contributing to the Warburg-associated diversion of glucose to biosynthesis.

relations.note: Glucose 6-phosphate is the shared entry metabolite: hexokinase-derived G6P from glycolysis is drawn into the oxidative PPP.
The non-oxidative branch returns carbon to glycolysis as fructose 6-phosphate via transaldolase and transketolase.
The non-oxidative branch returns carbon to glycolysis as glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate; conversely, glycolytic G3P/F6P can feed the branch in reverse to make ribose.
Ribose 5-phosphate is converted to PRPP, the ribose donor for de novo purine and pyrimidine nucleotide synthesis.
PPP-derived NADPH is the principal reductant for fatty-acid and sterol biosynthesis in liver and adipose tissue.
NADPH regenerates reduced glutathione via glutathione reductase, the main defense against oxidative stress — critical in erythrocytes lacking mitochondria.
